chunks.page_summary: This page provides an overview and initial preparation steps for field calibration, verification, and re-calibration of Rheonics Type-SR inline viscometers, including guidance on selecting reference standards and proper handling.
product_names: Rheonics Process Viscometer and Density Meter
chunks.extracted_text:
Phone: +1 713 234 0863
Calibration for the Type-SR sensors in field, verification and re-calibration
Modified on: Tue, 7 Oct, 2025 at 5:46 PM

TABLE OF CONTENTS
1. Overview
2. Preparations for Viscometer Calibration
3. Reference Calibration Fluids and Standards
4. Rheonics SRV Viscometer: Calibration Overview
5. Calibration/Verification Procedure for SRV Sensor
6. Calibration/Verification Procedure for SRD Sensor
7. Troubleshooting
8. ISO 17025 Certification Compliance
9. References

1. Overview
Rheonics sensors are shipped factory calibrated. They do not need re-calibration in field, however for various regulatory (ISO, FDA, others) fulfillment of requirements, it may be necessary to verify or calibrate the sensors periodically.
Rheonics inline viscometer: SRV (https://rheonics.com/products/inline-viscometer-srv/) measures a product of dynamic viscosity and density, called kinematic viscosity (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000413004). To compare the measured values against reference viscosity values from viscosity standard, you can follow the procedure from this article.

Figure 1. Rheonics SRV Inline Viscometer

This article mentions various third-party companies and organizations, their use here is by no means an endorsement of any product or company mentioned.
Users are responsible for making their own decisions regarding the selection and should review information from the respective suppliers to assess suitability.

2. Preparations for Viscometer Calibration
2.1 Choosing the Right Reference Viscosity Standard for Calibration
For obtaining suitable reference viscosity standards, we recommend the following companies and standard bodies that provide traceable viscosity standards:
*   Cannon Instruments (https://cannoninstrument.com/) is the supplier of NIST traceable viscosity standards, widely available in USA, Europe, Australia, Asia.
*   Nippon Grease standards, generally available in Japan
*   PTB (Physikalisch-Technische Bundesanstalt) is the national metrological body in Germany. Newtonian calibration liquids for viscosity are supplied by calibration laboratories accredited by Deutsche Akkreditierungsstelle GmbH (DAkkS). These liquids are traceable to the National Standard of Viscosity according to DIN EN ISO/IEC 17025.

2.2 Proper Handling and Preparation for Accurate Viscometer Calibration
To calibrate or verify the inline viscometer calibration, it is important to ensure the operator follows proper procedure for handling the sensor and calibration fluids while ensuring appropriate operational conditions.
*   Sensor should be fixed ideally using a clamp or sensor holder
*   Fluid should be poured in a calibration cylinder around the viscometer or in a beaker in which the sensor is dipped. For minimizing calibration fluid volume, Rheonics recommends using calibration stands and cylinders. Take a look at our article: Using Rheonics Process Viscometer and Density Meter as Tabletop Instruments (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000412598).
*   Calibration fluid should not have bubbles.
*   Let the temperature stabilize to ensure accurate measurements.
key_facts: Rheonics sensors are shipped factory calibrated and do not typically require field re-calibration, but periodic verification or calibration may be necessary for regulatory compliance (e.g., ISO, FDA).
The Rheonics SRV inline viscometer measures dynamic viscosity and density to determine kinematic viscosity.
For calibration, users can compare measured values against reference viscosity values from viscosity standards.
Recommended suppliers for traceable viscosity standards include Cannon Instruments, Nippon Grease, and PTB (Physikalisch-Technische Bundesanstalt).
PTB supplies Newtonian calibration liquids traceable to the National Standard of Viscosity according to DIN EN ISO/IEC 17025.
Proper handling of the sensor and calibration fluids is crucial for accurate calibration and verification.
The sensor should be fixed, ideally with a clamp or holder.
Calibration fluid should be poured around the viscometer in a calibration cylinder or beaker, with Rheonics recommending calibration stands and cylinders to minimize fluid volume.
Calibration fluid must not contain bubbles.
Allow temperature to stabilize for accurate measurements during calibration.
warnings_notes: This article mentions various third-party companies and organizations; their use here is by no means an endorsement of any product or company mentioned.
Users are responsible for making their own decisions regarding the selection and should review information from the respective suppliers to assess suitability.

chunks.page_summary: This page provides guidelines for calibrating Rheonics SRV viscometers and SRD viscosity-density sensors, detailing sensor setup, recommended calibration fluids, and an analysis of the limitations of silicone oils.
chunks.extracted_text:
This method only uses a beaker, which is fine for normal temperature calibration. For different temperature setpoints, Rheonics recommends the S
The following images illustrates the main considerations for SRV and SRD calibration.

Figure 2. Considerations for Rheonics Viscometer: SRV

Figure 3. Considerations for Rheonics Viscosity-density sensor: SRD

*If the process fluid temperature differs by more than 15 °C from the ambient temperature, then thermal equilibrium along the probe should be considered, i.e. an oven or a long insertion probe for deeper immersion is recommended.

Learn more about SRD's thermal equilibrium in this article: Maintaining the SRD's temperature balance for high density accuracy (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000412500).

3. Reference Calibration Fluids and Standards
Calibration fluids are essential for accurate viscometer and density meter calibration. These are generally provided with measured data by the calibration labs which follow ASTM, ISO, DIN, EN standards. However, there is a wide variety of calibration fluids available in the market. Below is a short analysis of the various available fluids and our recommendations.

3.1 Rotation Viscometer Calibration Fluids (Silicones)

Overview
Silicone oils are typically provided with a single kinematic value at a single temperature.

Limitations
* Like the rotation viscometers (e.g. Brookfield), these are very inaccurate test fluids and useless for any calibration since the fluid and rotational viscometers have both quite high inaccuracy
* Viscoelastic nature causes viscosity to vary depending on the frequency of oscillation.
* Results differ significantly when measured with vibrational instruments like the SRV, SRD, or DVP.

Recommendation
Avoid using silicone oils for calibration due to their inherent inaccuracies and inconsistencies.

3.2 Mineral Oils

Overview
key_facts: A beaker method is suitable for normal temperature calibration.
The sensing area of both SRV and SRD sensors must be fully immersed during calibration.
Mixing or stirring the fluid during calibration is optional, but a varying fluid level can impact readings.
Heaters are optional for testing at different temperatures for SRV and near ambient temperatures for SRD.
Thermal equilibrium should be considered for SRD calibration if the process fluid temperature differs by more than 15 °C from the ambient temperature.
Calibration fluids are critical for accurate viscometer and density meter calibration, with measured data typically provided by labs following ASTM, ISO, DIN, EN standards.
Silicone oils are generally provided with a single kinematic value at a single temperature.
Silicone oils are considered inaccurate test fluids for calibration due to inherent inaccuracies when used with rotational viscometers.
The viscoelastic nature of silicone oils causes their viscosity to vary with the frequency of oscillation.
Measurement results with silicone oils differ significantly when using vibrational instruments like SRV, SRD, or DVP.
Rheonics recommends avoiding silicone oils for calibration due to their inherent inaccuracies and inconsistencies.

chunks.page_summary: This page provides Rheonics' recommendations for using NIST traceable viscosity reference standards from Cannon Instruments for viscometer calibration, including key considerations, features, and an example Certificate of Analysis.
chunks.extracted_text:
Newtonian fluids, ideal for viscometer calibration.

Key Considerations
*   Often provided with single measurements at specific conditions.
*   For proper calibration or verification, obtain oils with both density and viscosity values measured at various temperatures.
*   Both density and dynamic viscosity of the oil are necessary to perform an accurate calibration check on the SRV.

3.3 Rheonics Recommendation: NIST Traceable Viscosity References

Overview
These viscosity references can be sourced from Cannon Instruments in the USA and worldwide. Other suppliers may also provide similar references. In this article, we primarily refer to calibration fluids sourced from Cannon Instruments.

Key Features
*   NIST Traceable Standards: These viscosity references adhere to national standards, ensuring high accuracy and consistency.
*   Wide Applicability: Suitable for calibration across various viscosity ranges.

Additional Resources
*   NIST Traceable Viscosity Standards (https://cannoninstrument.com/viscosity-flash-point-standards/general-purpose-viscosity-standards.html): Available directly from Cannon Instruments.
*   Viscosity Reference Selection (https://cannoninstrument.com/general-dynamic-viscosity-table): Based on the specific target range of your application.

chunks.page_summary: This page outlines the calibration overview and step-by-step procedure for the Rheonics SRV Viscometer, including fluid handling, temperature stabilization, data recording, and accuracy calculation.
chunks.extracted_text:
4. Rheonics SRV Viscometer: Calibration Overview

*   Measurement Principle
    *   The SRV sensor measures the product of dynamic viscosity and density.

*   Default Density Setting
    *   The SRV sensor has a default density value of 1.000.

*   Recommended Calibration Fluids
    *   For typical viscosity ranges: Use any two of the following:
        *   S20, S60, S200, S600
    *   For higher viscosity ranges:
        *   N4000

*   Handling High Viscosity Fluids
    *   Pour fluids like S200, S600, N4000 carefully into the measurement container to avoid air entrapment.
    *   To remove bubbles, heat the fluid to 80°C, then cool it to the target temperature before calibration.

*   Required Accessories
    *   Sensor Holders & Calibration Cylinders: Rheonics provides tools specifically designed for secure and precise calibration setups. Review our Accessories Product Page (https://rheonics.com/product-accessories/) more information.
    *   Temperature Control System (STCM): For performing multi-point temperature ramps, ideal for both calibration and testing. Learn more at STCM (https://rheonics.com/product-accessories/rheonics-stcm-line/) page.

5. Calibration/Verification Procedure for SRV Sensor

5.1 Prepare the Calibration Setup
*   Get the appropriate viscosity reference fluid.
*   Use a beaker to hold the fluid and secure the sensor in a clamp or Rheonics sensor holder. Avoid holding the sensor by hand.
*   Ensure the sensor is not touching the side or bottom of the beaker. If using a Rheonics calibration cylinder (https://rheonics.com/product-accessories/scc-srx/), insert it fully with an O-ring at the bottom.

5.2 Fill the Calibration Fluid
*   Pour the fluid into the beaker, ensuring minimal bubble formation. Getting rid of bubbles is critical to achieve a good calibration.

5.3 Stabilize Temperature
*   Allow the sensor and fluid to stabilize until the temperature variation is less than 0.1°C (sensor output). This may take between 30–60 minutes unless an oven or STCM is being used.

5.4 Verify the Density Setting
*   Check that the SRV density is set to 1.0 (default setting).

5.5 Record and Compare Data
*   Record five viscosity readings from the SRV sensor. Ensure that the variance between these readings is less than 1%.
*   Compare the SRV readings to the reference fluid value. Usually, for precise viscosity values at specific temperatures, a software such as Viscdisk from Cannon Instruments needs to be used. For other suppliers, contact them directly.

5.6 Calculate Calibration Accuracy
*   Use the formula to calculate accuracy:
    Calibration Accuracy (%) = Measured Viscosity (SRV) / (Dynamic Viscosity * Density)

5.7 Adjust for Non-Default Density
*   If the density is set to a different value, input the correct density from the calibration reference data into the SRV.
*   The SRV will now output dynamic viscosity, which can then be compared against the reference fluid's dynamic viscosity.
key_facts: The SRV sensor measures the product of dynamic viscosity and density.
The SRV sensor has a default density value of 1.000.
Rheonics provides sensor holders and calibration cylinders designed for secure and precise calibration setups.
The Temperature Control System (STCM) is ideal for performing multi-point temperature ramps for calibration and testing.
Getting rid of bubbles during fluid filling is critical to achieve a good calibration.
The sensor and fluid should stabilize until the temperature variation is less than 0.1°C (sensor output) before calibration.
The temperature stabilization process may take between 30 to 60 minutes unless an oven or STCM is used.
The variance between five viscosity readings from the SRV sensor should be less than 1%.
For precise viscosity values at specific temperatures, software like Viscdisk from Cannon Instruments may be required.
Calibration Accuracy (%) is calculated by dividing the Measured Viscosity (SRV) by the product of Dynamic Viscosity and Density.
If the density is set to a non-default value, the correct density from the calibration reference data must be input into the SRV to obtain dynamic viscosity output.
warnings_notes: Pour high viscosity fluids like S200, S600, N4000 carefully into the measurement container to avoid air entrapment.
To remove bubbles from high viscosity fluids, heat the fluid to 80°C, then cool it to the target temperature before calibration.
Avoid holding the sensor by hand when securing it in a clamp or Rheonics sensor holder.
Ensure the sensor is not touching the side or bottom of the beaker during calibration setup.
Ensure minimal bubble formation when pouring the calibration fluid into the beaker, as getting rid of bubbles is critical for a good calibration.

chunks.page_summary: This page details procedures for rechecking calibration accuracy, calibration and verification for SRD sensors, troubleshooting common issues, compliance with ISO 17025 certification, and references for viscosity standards.
chunks.extracted_text:
5.8 Recheck Calibration Accuracy
*   Compare the measured viscosity by SRV with the product of dynamic viscosity and density from the calibration fluid bottle. If the product is not used, the measured viscosity will appear much lower than expected.

6. Calibration/Verification Procedure for SRD Sensor
The calibration procedure for the SRD sensor follows the same steps as outlined for the SRV, with the following additional points:

1.  The SRD sensor measures both kinematic viscosity and dynamic viscosity, so the measurements must be compared to the corresponding reference values on the fluid bottle.
2.  Once the sensor readings have stabilized:
    *   Take five readings of viscosity and density, ensuring variance between readings is less than 1%.
    *   Compare the values to the reference fluid data.
3.  Estimate calibration accuracy using these formulas:

Dynamic Viscosity Accuracy (%) = Measured Viscosity (SRD) / (Reference Viscosity × 100)

Density Accuracy (%) = Measured Density (SRD) / (Reference Density × 100)

7. Troubleshooting

7.1 Inaccurate Readings
*   Issue: The sensor’s sensing element is not completely submerged, leading to inaccurate readings.
*   Solution: Make sure sensor sensing element is completely submerged in the fluid. The picture below shows a potential issue since the sensing element is not completely in the fluid.

7.2 Unstable Viscosity Readings
*   Issue: The viscosity values are inconsistent because the fluid and sensor temperatures have not stabilized.
*   Solution: Wait long enough to stabilize temperature or use an oven or STCM.

7.3 SRV Viscosity doesn’t match with Calibration Fluid
*   Issue: The SRV viscosity output is only being compared to the dynamic viscosity of the calibration fluids, ignoring the density value.
*   Solution: Compare SRV viscosity output to the product of dynamic viscosity and density if the calibration fluid density has not been entered in the SRV electronics.

If the default density output of 1.000 g/cc is shown, ensure dynamic viscosity is multiplied by density for accurate comparison. (Refer to the R

8. ISO 17025 Certification Compliance

For factory calibration and calibration verification Rheonics uses NIST traceable viscosity and density reference fluids from Cannon Instruments. Cannon Instrument holds ISO 17025 and 17034 certifications for calibration and production of references. These certificates can be accessed from Cannon Instrument website.

When a calibration certificate is required, ensure it is ordered prior to shipment. CUS option in the calibration is required for the calibration certificate that contains information on the Reference fluid and Lot number.

9. References

1. Cannon Instruments NIST Newtonian Viscosity Standards
    *   View Brochure (https://www.cannoninstrument.com/media/assets/product/documents/standards.brochure.pdf)
    *   Also available as an attachment.
2. PTB Newtonian Calibration Liquids for Viscosity
key_facts: The measured viscosity by SRV should be compared with the product of dynamic viscosity and density from the calibration fluid bottle; otherwise, the measured viscosity will appear much lower than expected.
The SRD sensor measures both kinematic and dynamic viscosity.
For SRD sensor calibration, five readings of viscosity and density should be taken once stabilized, ensuring variance between readings is less than 1%.
Readings must be compared to reference fluid data.
Inaccurate readings can be caused by the sensor’s sensing element not being completely submerged.
Unstable viscosity readings indicate that fluid and sensor temperatures have not stabilized.
If SRV viscosity output is only compared to dynamic viscosity, it may not match calibration fluid; density must also be considered.
Rheonics utilizes NIST traceable viscosity and density reference fluids from Cannon Instruments for factory calibration and verification.
Cannon Instrument holds ISO 17025 and 17034 certifications.
A calibration certificate requires a CUS option in calibration and must be ordered prior to shipment.
warnings_notes: If the sensor's sensing element is not completely submerged, it leads to inaccurate readings.
Inconsistent viscosity values indicate unstabilized fluid and sensor temperatures.
If the default density output of 1.000 g/cc is shown, dynamic viscosity must be multiplied by density for accurate comparison.
